context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m working on Problem Set 6 for 6.1200, and I’m pretty stuck on the graph theory parts, especially Problems 1 and 2. Could we start with Problem 1(a)? I have a vague feeling the counterexample is some complete graph, but I’m not fully seeing it.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct! Let's test it. For a complete graph K_n, what are its chromatic number and its number of edges? Does it satisfy the condition of having at most 3n − 6 edges?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuition about complete graphs. Rather than confirming or denying it directly, I should ask a Socratic question that pushes them to work out the details themselves: what do they know about complete graphs in terms of edges and chromatic number? This will guide them toward checking whether a complete graph satisfies the edge constraint and fails 6-coloring.

exchanges.student: I think \(K_n\) has chromatic number \(n\), since every vertex needs a different color. Its number of edges is \(\binom{n}{2} = n(n-1)/2\). I’m not sure which \(n\) makes \(n(n-1)/2 \le 3n-6\) while still needing more than 6 colors, though.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right so far. So just solve the inequality you wrote: for which values of n does n(n−1)/2 ≤ 3n−6 hold? Try simplifying it algebraically.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both the chromatic number and edge count of K_n. Now I should guide them to solve the inequality they themselves wrote down. This is a straightforward algebraic step that the student can do on their own. I'll prompt them to solve it.

exchanges.student:
Okay, let me try:  
\[
\frac{n(n-1)}{2} \le 3n-6
\]
so
\[
n(n-1) \le 6n-12
\]
and then
\[
n^2-7n+12 \le 0.
\]
I think that factors as \((n-3)(n-4)\le 0\), so that would mean \(3 \le n \le 4\)? If that’s right, then a complete graph won’t work for needing more than 6 colors, so I guess my first idea fails.
exchanges.tutor: Good work on the algebra — your conclusion is right, K_n won't work. So step back: what are the two things your counterexample graph needs to satisfy simultaneously?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly solved the inequality and reached the right conclusion that complete graphs won't work as counterexamples. This is good progress. Now I should nudge them to think differently: maybe the counterexample doesn't have to be a single well-known graph type. I should ask them to think about what properties the counterexample needs to satisfy, without giving away the answer.

exchanges.student: It needs to have at most \(3n-6\) edges, and it also needs to not be 6-colorable, so its chromatic number has to be at least 7. I’m guessing I need a graph with relatively few edges compared to a complete graph, but still chromatic number 7.
exchanges.tutor: Good framing. Here's a question to focus your search: what's the simplest or most natural graph you know that has chromatic number exactly 7? Check whether it satisfies the edge constraint.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the two requirements. Now I should guide them toward thinking about what the simplest graph with chromatic number exactly 7 looks like, without giving it away. The most natural candidate is K_7, but they've already shown that doesn't satisfy the edge constraint. However, K_7 has exactly 3*7-6=15 edges... wait, let me check: K_7 has 7*6/2 = 21 edges, and 3*7-6 = 15. So K_7 doesn't work. But actually, what about graphs that aren't complete but still have chromatic number 7? The student should think about this. Actually, the simplest graph with chromatic number 7 is K_7 itself. But since complete graphs don't work for n>4, they need a different construction. A key insight: what if we think about it differently? Any graph requiring 7 colors must contain an odd cycle or something more complex. Actually, the most straightforward counterexample might be to think about complete graphs more carefully - K_7 has 21 edges and 3(7)-6=15, so it doesn't satisfy the constraint. We need a graph with χ≥7 but few edges. Actually, I should let the student think about this. One natural approach: think about what graph constructions give high chromatic number with few edges. But I shouldn't over-scaffold. Let me just ask them to think about what the minimum number of edges a graph with chromatic number 7 could have.

exchanges.student: The most natural one I know is \(K_7\), but that has \(21\) edges, and \(3n-6 = 15\) when \(n=7\), so it fails. So I guess I need some non-complete 7-chromatic graph. I don’t know many examples off the top of my head though.
exchanges.tutor: You're close. What if you kept K_7 as a subgraph but added more isolated vertices to your graph? How would that affect n, the edge count, and the chromatic number?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck because they're thinking they need a non-complete 7-chromatic graph with few edges, which is a harder construction. But actually, they might be overcomplicating this. The key insight they might be missing: the problem says 'at most 3n-6 edges' but n is the number of nodes in THEIR graph. They could use a graph with more than 7 nodes that still requires 7 colors but has fewer edges relative to its node count. The simplest such construction: take K_7 and add isolated vertices. Adding isolated vertices increases n without increasing edges or decreasing chromatic number. This is the natural hint to give - ask them what happens if they add nodes to K_7.

exchanges.student: Ohh, I think that works. If I take \(K_7\) plus some isolated vertices, the chromatic number stays \(7\) because the \(K_7\) is still there, and isolated vertices don’t force new colors. The number of edges stays \(21\), while \(n\) increases, so eventually \(21 \le 3n-6\).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right! So what's the smallest n that makes 21 ≤ 3n−6 work? That gives you your concrete counterexample.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right idea. They just need to find the specific n that works. I should ask them to compute it explicitly so their answer is concrete and complete.

exchanges.student: I get \(21 \le 3n-6\), so \(27 \le 3n\), hence \(n \ge 9\). So the smallest example should be a graph on 9 vertices made of \(K_7\) plus 2 isolated vertices. That has 21 edges, and \(3\cdot 9-6=21\), and it still can’t be 6-colored because the \(K_7\) part already needs 7 colors.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid counterexample — well done. Now for part (b): why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? What step in that argument fails when you try to apply it to 6-coloring instead of the original claim?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the counterexample with all the right reasoning. This is a complete and correct answer for part (a). I should affirm this and transition them to part (b), which asks why the induction argument breaks down for this stronger claim.

exchanges.student:
I’m a little fuzzy on the recitation proof, but I think it used the fact that a graph with at most \(3n-6\) edges has some vertex of small degree, then removes that vertex and colors the rest inductively, and finally colors the removed vertex.

Maybe the issue is that “small degree” there only guarantees degree \(\le 5\)? And for 6-coloring I guess that sounds like it should still be enough, so I’m not seeing exactly where it breaks.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you have the proof structure right. Now focus on the inductive step: after you remove a vertex, you apply the inductive hypothesis to the remaining graph. For that to work, what condition must the remaining graph satisfy, and is that guaranteed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right structure of the proof in mind but is confused about where it breaks. They correctly identified that the degree bound gives ≤5, which seems fine for 6-coloring. The real issue is about what happens when you remove a vertex: does the resulting subgraph still satisfy the edge constraint 3n-6 for the smaller graph? I should guide them to check whether the inductive hypothesis still applies to the subgraph after removing a vertex.

exchanges.student: Oh, I think I see the problem now. After removing a vertex, I’d need the smaller graph to still satisfy the stronger hypothesis “has at most \(3m-6\) edges” so I can apply induction to it, and that isn’t guaranteed if the removed vertex had small degree in the wrong direction. I’m still a bit confused on the exact inequality check after deleting a degree-5-or-less vertex.
exchanges.tutor: Work it out concretely: if the original graph has at most 3n−6 edges and you remove a vertex of degree d, how many edges remain? And what does the constraint require for an (n−1)-vertex graph?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but needs to work through the specific inequality. I should prompt them to do the concrete calculation: if the original graph has n vertices and at most 3n-6 edges, and you remove a vertex of degree d, how many edges does the remaining (n-1)-vertex graph have, and what does the constraint 3(n-1)-6 require?

exchanges.student: If I remove a vertex of degree \(d\), then the remaining graph has at most \((3n-6)-d\) edges. For induction on \(n-1\) vertices, I’d need that to be at most \(3(n-1)-6 = 3n-9\). So I guess I need \((3n-6)-d \le 3n-9\), which means \(d \ge 3\). That’s the part that feels weird, because the argument was giving me an upper bound like \(d \le 5\), not a lower bound.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — you've found the gap. So what vertex degree would cause the induction to break down, and does your counterexample from part (a) actually contain such a vertex?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done the calculation correctly and identified the exact issue: the induction requires d ≥ 3, but the argument only gives an upper bound d ≤ 5. They need to connect this to the counterexample: isolated vertices (d=0) or low-degree vertices can violate this. I should ask them to connect this algebraic finding to why the induction breaks down - specifically, what degree could vertices have that violates d ≥ 3?

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, guiding the student to derive each key insight themselves rather than providing answers directly. The student did the algebraic work, identified the counterexample structure, and worked out the inductive step failure — all with the tutor asking focused questions rather than supplying conclusions. No rubric violations were identified.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a direct answer or near-submission-ready output. Each turn posed a focused question that prompted the student to do the reasoning (e.g., 'solve the inequality you wrote,' 'work it out concretely'). The student derived the counterexample and the induction gap themselves. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on the student's correct progress (e.g., affirming the algebra in Turn 3, building on the isolated-vertex idea in Turn 6) and asked diagnostic questions before redirecting. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, pointing the student toward the specific inequality check and the structure of the inductive step rather than just saying 'good' or 'wrong.' 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation stayed on-task and progressed without repetition or drift. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, focused, and supportive throughout, with no monologues or tone issues.
